role_summary: Baltimore CORE organizer; SNCC field secretary in West Point, Mississippi and Phillips County, Arkansas (1960s); first executive director of the Avery Research Center for African American History and Culture at the College of Charleston (1985-1993); the founding figure of the Avery Research Center's Lowcountry-Gullah-and-Sierra-Leone diaspora-connection scholarship program.
ai_reading: Glascoe's neighbor counts are moderate (peak 86), with Charles McLaurin (entry 17) as the top match and the broader cluster including Harold K. Brown (entry 55), Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (entry 53), and John Churchville (entry 63). The novel observation her page surfaces is the field-organizer-to-archive-director transit signature: Glascoe's 1960s SNCC field work in West Point Mississippi and Phillips County Arkansas, followed by her doctorate at Harvard, then her 1985-1993 founding directorship of the Avery Research Center, represents a movement-to-institutional-memory career arc the catalog tracks in only a handful of figures (Judy Richardson into documentary production, Vincent Harding into theological-and-historical-scholarship, Doris Derby into Georgia State African American Studies); the embedding catches her testimony as continuous with the broader SNCC field tradition, the Avery Research Center director role reading as an archive-of-the-organizing-tradition rather than as a separate institutional career.
biographical_paragraph: While Glascoe's LoC oral history carries an ingestion-only audit tier (the catalog ingested the transcript but did not subject it to the audit cascade, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript itself should be confirmed against external archives before citation), her external biographical record is documented in the Veterans of the Civil Rights Movement archive and the institutional records of the Avery Research Center [src: 1, src: 2]. Glascoe grew up in Washington, D.C., attended Howard University and the University of Pennsylvania, and had an early career in education and social work. She joined the Baltimore Congress of Racial Equality (CORE), relocated to California, and worked as a SNCC field secretary in West Point, Mississippi and Phillips County, Arkansas in the mid-1960s [src: 2]. After leaving the South she earned a doctorate in education at Harvard University. In 1985 she became the first executive director of the Avery Research Center for African American History and Culture at the College of Charleston, the historic Lowcountry African American educational and cultural institution; she served eight years across 1985-1993, hosting the Center's first 1986 conference (on Black women in the South) and establishing the Center's Gullah-and-Sierra-Leone diaspora-scholarship program that has been the institutional anchor of the Avery's subsequent forty-year work [src: 2].

interview_snippets.speaker: Myrtle Gonza Glascoe
interview_snippets.lead_in: Glascoe describes the Baltimore CORE sit-in at the White Coffee Pot, a restaurant that barred Black customers, as the moment her involvement shifted:
interview_snippets.quote: And so I went with them to the White Coffee Pot and as soon as we sat down there was this guy who got up and the guy came to us, he had grease on him it seemed like from the top of his head to the bottom of his feet, dirty apron, greasy hair and he read this thing to us which said we had to get up and go. And so the intention was that we would get up and go and so we got up and we left. And I was so mad, I could feel that anger all the way home, I could feel the anger. And I just-anyway it was like a rod of steel was put up my back and I can't even describe how I felt, it was that burn that turned me on.

interview_snippets.speaker: Myrtle Gonza Glascoe
interview_snippets.lead_in: Working with the SNCC support network in San Francisco, Glascoe recounts driving Fannie Lou Hamer to a workshop:
interview_snippets.quote: One time we had a workshop and Mrs. Hamer came and I had to drive her around in my car and I was so excited, I was just thrilled. And she was just as down to earth as she could be. As soon we got away from all the folks she pulled out her tobacco and she stuck it in her mouth and she says, "Now I can get me a good chew."

interview_snippets.speaker: Myrtle Gonza Glascoe
interview_snippets.lead_in: Glascoe recalls the instruction the project director in West Helena, Arkansas gave her about how a newly arrived field worker should conduct herself in community meetings:
interview_snippets.quote: And as a matter of fact the director of the project in West Helena told me go to community meetings but do not get up and tell people what you think, stay in your seat. If you have to write down what you think and we can talk about it later. And so that was the most important experience for me that summer.

interview_snippets.speaker: Myrtle Gonza Glascoe
interview_snippets.lead_in: Describing her work in Marvell, Arkansas with Gertrude Jackson, Glascoe explains the organizing method she arrived at, having local leaders own and present their own ideas rather than channeling them through her:
interview_snippets.quote: And so the way she would do it is she would tell me the idea and so I would be so excited about it I would just go to the meeting and when the meeting was going on I would present the idea. Then I finally-I backed up a little bit and I thought about it and I said to myself, Mrs. Jackson is planting stuff on me and she should be doing it herself.

interview_snippets.speaker: Myrtle Gonza Glascoe
interview_snippets.lead_in: Glascoe recounts the 1985 invitation to direct the Avery Research Center, describing the history of the Avery Normal Institute building that would house it:
interview_snippets.quote: I was invited in 1985 to be the Founding Director of the Avery Research Center for African American History and Culture. It was envisioned as in archives by a group of people who had attended the Avery School-like the Avery School was founded in 1865 and it was the first academic high school for black people in Charleston, South Carolina. And it remained open as an academic high school for black people until 1954.

interview_snippets.speaker: Gertrude Newsome Jackson
interview_snippets.lead_in: Gertrude Jackson, the Marvell, Arkansas matriarch whose family anchored the local project, recalls Glascoe coming to her home:
interview_snippets.quote: What I remember about Myrtle? Well, she'd come to the house and they'd just say things, they'd just--just like another one of my 11 children. And just remember her, just point something, up and come into the house and sit right at the table with all my 10 children and like I say, just like another daughter, she would go to the meetings with us and you know, they would explain different things about civil rights and things like that.

interview_snippets.speaker: Carrie Lamar Young
interview_snippets.lead_in: Carrie Young, a fellow Phillips County, Arkansas organizer with whom Glascoe boarded, describes the bond they formed:
interview_snippets.quote: She became my big sister that I had lost like a year or two before she showed up. And she wound up staying with us and we got close and close.

gallery.alt: The Avery Research Center for African American History and Culture at 125 Bull Street, Charleston, South Carolina
gallery.caption: The Avery Research Center for African American History and Culture at 125 Bull Street, Charleston, South Carolina, photographed in 2019. The building housed the Avery Normal Institute, founded in 1865 as an academic high school for Black students in Charleston and supported by the American Missionary Association until it closed as a school in 1954. Glascoe was the Center's founding director from 1985 to 1993, and in her oral history she recounts that the building was deteriorating and unusable when she arrived and describes acquiring the Guy and Candy Carawan collection of John's Island music as the Center's first purchase.
